payload.topic:
SKILLS MANAGEMENT ARCHITECTURE FOR SOLO-DEVELOPER AI AGENT ECOSYSTEM

CONTEXT: HestAI is a solo-developer system with 30+ agents, 65+ skills, and 13+ patterns. Each agent's .oct.md file contains a §3::CAPABILITIES section declaring CHASSIS (always-loaded skills) and PROFILES (context-specific skill sets with match conditions). Skills live as files in .hestai-sys/library/skills/.

THE PROBLEM: When you rename, deprecate, or restructure a skill, you must update every agent file that references it. With 30 agents and growing, this is a many-to-many relationship that creates friction. During a recent batch of agent interviews, we created 14 new skills and rewrote 3 agent files — each requiring manual skill-to-profile mapping.

THREE OPTIONS ALREADY CONSIDERED:
A) Central database (Workbench SQLite) — breaks hermetic state, governance leaves Git
B) Global skill-matrix.oct.md file — breaks holographic contract (agent file is no longer self-contained DNA)  
C) Keep in agent files (current) — high verbosity, multi-file grep-and-replace for refactors, but declaratively auditable and Git-diffable

ADDITIONAL CONTEXT:
- This is a SOLO DEVELOPER system — no team coordination overhead
- Agent files are consumed by the Odyssean Anchor ceremony which reads §3::CAPABILITIES to load skills
- The anchor server already parses agent files programmatically
- We have octave_write MCP tool that can programmatically write .oct.md files
- We have a Workbench (Electron app) that could provide UI abstractions
- Skills can be loaded as full-body or kernel_only depending on profile
- The system already has a skill-creator skill and skills-expert agent for authoring

QUESTION: Is there a better architecture for managing the Agent↔Skill relationship that preserves Git auditability and holographic self-containment while reducing the friction of ecosystem-wide skill refactoring? Consider approaches that no one has proposed yet. Think beyond the three options. This is a solo developer who can do whatever they want — no enterprise constraints, no team politics, just the best possible architecture for one person orchestrating 30+ AI agents.
